translatedText: Born in Königsberg, a commercial city in Prussia.
Entered the Collegium Fridericianum, a private school directed by theologian Franz Albert Schultz, who was an acquaintance of his mother.
Graduated from the Collegium Fridericianum and entered the University of Königsberg to study philosophy and mathematics.
His father died, and he published his graduation thesis, 'Thoughts on the True Estimation of Living Forces'.

Received his doctorate with a dissertation titled 'Universal Natural History and Theory of the Heavens' and obtained the qualification to lecture at the university with the thesis 'A New Elucidation of the First Principles of Metaphysical Cognition'.
Declined a literature professorship offered by the Prussian Ministry of Education.
Took a job as a sub-librarian at the Royal Library to cover his living expenses.
Secured a professorship in philosophy at the University of Königsberg and delivered his inaugural dissertation.
Finished his employment as a librarian at the Royal Library.
Published the first edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the essays 'Answering the Question: What Is Enlightenment?' and 'Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysic of Morals'.
Published 'Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science' and was elected rector of the University of Königsberg, serving until 1788.
Published the second edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published 'Critique of Practical Reason'.
Published 'Critique of Judgment'.
Had a conflict with the faculty administration over the publication and censorship of his essay 'On the Radical Evil in Human Nature'.
Published 'Religion within the Boundaries of Mere Reason'.
Published 'Perpetual Peace: A Philosophical Sketch'.
Published the 'Metaphysics of Morals'.
Passed away at the age of 80 on February 12 at 4:00 AM.
Establishment of the foundation of German idealism
He brought modern Enlightenment to its peak and established the foundation of German idealism.
Establishment of critical philosophy
Established critical philosophy to overcome conventional empiricism and dogmatism.
Authorship of 'Critique of Pure Reason'
Studied the structure and limits of reason itself to attack traditional metaphysics and epistemology and highlighted his own contributions.
Authorship of 'Critique of Practical Reason'
Focused on ethics and found the basis of morality in a transcendental freedom not governed by causality.
Authorship of 'Critique of Judgment'
Presented the essence of critical philosophy by researching aesthetics and teleology.
Attempted a Copernican revolution
Pointed out the limits of human reason and attempted a 'Copernican revolution' by introducing a priori forms into human cognition.
Presentation of the categorical imperative
Valued the motive more than the outcome of an action, proposing the categorical imperative as an absolute command of duty to be followed unconditionally under any circumstance.
Proposal of perpetual peace
Proposed an international league based on the concept of international law to realize a perpetual peace without war between states.
What are Kant's major works?
Kant's major works include 'Critique of Pure Reason', 'Critique of Practical Reason', and 'Critique of Judgment'.
What does the 'Copernican revolution' mean in Kant's epistemology?
The 'Copernican revolution' is the idea that humans do not perceive objects exactly as they are, but rather that human cognition constructs the concept of the object.
What are the two commands of the moral law proposed by Kant?

The first is to act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law. The second is to act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end.
What is unique about Kant's life?
Kant never traveled more than 100 miles away from Königsberg in his entire life and maintained a highly regular daily routine.
Born on March 31 into a family of aristocratic lawyers in La Haye en Touraine.
Enrolled in the Jesuit Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and was educated there until 1614.
Obtained his licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Solved a difficult geometry problem presented by the head of a Dutch university in Paris.
Traveled to the Netherlands where he met Isaac Beeckman and wrote his first work, the brief 'Compendium of Music'.
Joined the army of Maximilian of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a series of dreams in a military camp near Ulm that illuminated the path of his life.
Left the army and returned to France.
Began studying mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Penned fragments of 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind' and moved to the Netherlands that winter in search of freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote the book 'The World' (Le Monde) concerning the natural sciences.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', appending three essays on dioptrics, meteorology, and geometry, and published it anonymously.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy' (Meditationes).
Systematized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Published his final book, 'The Passions of the Soul' (Les passions de l'âme), derived from letters sent to Princess Elisabeth of the Palatinate, and went to the Swedish royal court at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Passed away from pneumonia in Stockholm on February 11 at the age of 54.
His remains were returned to Paris and interred in the Abbey of Saint-Geneviève.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were again interred in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.
Establishment of the foundation of modern philosophy
He is regarded as the founder of modern philosophy by establishing the basic framework of modern thought for the first time.
Invention of analytic geometry
Devised the Cartesian coordinate system, inventing analytic geometry which systematically integrated algebra and geometry.
Establishment of 'I think, therefore I am'
In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he established for the first time the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.
Advocacy of mind-body dualism
Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, soul and body, thereby connecting scientific naturalism with the metaphysics of the mind.
Discovery of the law of refraction of light
Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's law'.
Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy for first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he paved the way for modern mathematics by inventing analytic geometry.
What is Descartes' most famous proposition?

In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he famously established the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject' with the statement, 'I think, therefore I am' (Cogito ergo sum).
What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
He maintained a lifelong Roman Catholic faith and asserted that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.
What is Descartes' dualism?
He divided substance into two kinds: the mental ('thinking thing') and the material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are distinctly separate substances.
Born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland, to Isaac Rousseau, a watchmaker, and Suzanne Bernard.
His mother, Suzanne Bernard, died from complications of childbirth.
His father moved after a dispute with the city of Geneva, and Rousseau entered a boarding school near Geneva.
Began vocational training to become a court clerk.
Received Roman Catholic baptism in Turin, Italy, and was given the baptismal name Jean-Joseph.
Moved to Lausanne and worked as a music teacher under a pseudonym.
Visited Paris for the first time, then lived in places like Chambéry via Lyon.
Lived beside Madame de Warens in Chambéry and Les Charmettes, devoting himself to music and cultivating himself through reading.
Met and befriended the Enlightenment figures d'Alembert and Diderot in Paris.
Organized a new musical notation system and presented it at the French Academy of Sciences, but it was not successful.
After 'Dissertation sur la musique moderne' was published, he moved to Venice to serve as secretary to the Comte de Montaigu.
Married the maid Marie-Thérèse Levasseur after a long period of cohabitation.
Made a name for himself when his 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences' won first prize in an essay contest held by the Academy of Dijon, and his opera 'The Village Soothsayer' was performed.
Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy, and published 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences'.
